question_text: A rope is tied to a wall and shaken to create a wave. Explain what would happen to the wavelength if the rope is shaken faster (higher frequency) but the rope is moved the same distance up and down.
correct_answer: The wavelength would decrease (become shorter). The wave speed stays the same (determined by the rope properties), so if frequency increases, wavelength must decrease to maintain: wave speed = wavelength × frequency.
explanation: The wave speed depends on the medium (the rope), not on how fast you shake it. Since wave speed = wavelength × frequency, if you increase frequency, the wavelength must decrease. This creates more waves in the same space.
